module_name: Australia and New Zealand Laser Regulations
learning_objectives: Identify regulated laser classes in different Australian states
Recognize New Zealand laser safety standards
Understand licensing requirements for laser use

content.overview: Australian laser safety is governed by a combination of federal standards and state-specific regulations. Unlike some countries with uniform national laser laws, Australia has a decentralized regulatory approach.

key_authorities.name: ARPANSA
key_authorities.full_name: Australian Radiation Protection and Nuclear Safety Agency
key_authorities.role: Federal guidance on non-ionizing radiation including lasers

key_authorities.name: Safe Work Australia
key_authorities.role: National policy body for workplace health and safety
key_authorities.resource: Laser Classification and Potential Hazards documentation

regulated_states.description: Only three Australian states have specific regulations for powerful lasers and IPLs (Intense Pulsed Light devices):

states.name: Western Australia
states.regulations: Regulates Class 3B and Class 4 lasers regardless of purpose
states.notes: Class 4 lasers are commonly used for skin pigmentation treatment and hair removal. Beauty therapists can use less powerful lasers legally.

states.name: Queensland
states.regulations: Governed by Radiation Safety Act 1999 (Qld) and Radiation Safety Regulations 1999
requirements: Businesses must submit details of laser types to Queensland Health Authority
Required: Radiation Safety and Protection Plan for Diagnostic, Therapeutic or Cosmetic practices

states.name: Tasmania
requirements: License required for Class 3B or Class 4 lasers and IPLs used for medical purposes
Cosmetic procedures may soon require same licensing as medical use

australian_standard.title: Safe use of lasers in health care
australian_standard.status: Recognized standard but NOT law or regulation
australian_standard.requirements: All personnel using lasers should have training appropriate to their tasks

key_takeaways: Only WA, QLD, and TAS have specific laser regulations
Class 3B and Class 4 lasers are the primary regulated classes
IPL devices are regulated alongside lasers in these states
AS/NZS 4173:2004 provides guidance but is not legally binding
License requirements vary by state and application (medical vs cosmetic)

quiz_questions.question: Which Australian states currently have specific regulations for Class 3B and Class 4 lasers?
options: New South Wales, Victoria, and South Australia
All states have identical laser regulations
quiz_questions.explanation: Western Australia, Queensland, and Tasmania are the only states with specific regulations for powerful lasers and IPLs.

quiz_questions.question: Under Queensland's Radiation Safety Act 1999, what must businesses submit to the Queensland Health Authority?
options: Details of the type of laser being used
quiz_questions.explanation: Queensland requires businesses to submit details of the type of laser being used to the Queensland Health Authority.

quiz_questions.question: Is AS/NZS 4173:2004 legally binding in Australia?
quiz_questions.explanation: AS/NZS 4173:2004 is a recognized standard but is NOT law or regulation - it provides guidance that personnel should have appropriate training.

glossary.ARPANSA: Australian Radiation Protection and Nuclear Safety Agency - federal guidance body
glossary.IPL: Intense Pulsed Light - a broad-spectrum light source used for various cosmetic and medical treatments
glossary.AS/NZS 4173:2004: Australian/New Zealand Standard for safe use of lasers in health care
